case_profile.objective: Generate a mechanistic hypothesis for MET exon 14 skipping mutation as a therapeutic target in NSCLC. Synthesize: (1) the clinical precedence for MET inhibitors capmatinib and tepotinib in MET exon 14-positive NSCLC, including response rates and durability from pivotal trials; (2) the biological distinction between MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification as predictive biomarkers — mechanism, predictive value, and cross-response; (3) acquired resistance mechanisms including secondary MET kinase domain mutations D1228N, D1228H, and Y1230C, and bypass resistance via KRAS mutation and EGFR upregulation; (4) patient stratification strategies differentiating exon 14 skipping driver tumors from MET amplification secondary to EGFR resistance; and (5) ranked validation experiments for combination strategies addressing the most common resistance mechanisms.

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated oncogenic driver in NSCLC with established mechanistic rationale and regulatory-approved targeted therapies; the scientific frontier lies in resistance biology and rational combination strategies
key_claims: MET exon 14 skipping eliminates the juxtamembrane CBL-binding site encoded by exon 14, impairing ubiquitin-mediated receptor degradation and causing sustained MET signaling independent of ligand dose
Capmatinib (GEOMETRY mono-1) and tepotinib (VISION) achieved ~41-68% ORR in MET exon14-positive NSCLC with median PFS ~5.4-8.5 months, establishing clinical precedence

MET exon 14 skipping is a primary driver alteration; MET amplification arising after EGFR-TKI therapy is a bypass resistance mechanism with distinct biology and lower single-agent MET inhibitor response rates

Secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C sterically clash with type Ib inhibitor binding, conferring on-target resistance; KRAS mutation and EGFR upregulation represent off-target bypass resistance
Combination strategies pairing MET inhibitors with EGFR inhibitors or SHP2 inhibitors are mechanistically prioritized to address bypass resistance in exon 14-skipping tumors
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records for capmatinib and tepotinib in NSCLC confirming regulatory validation
NCBI Gene MET entry confirming RTK biology, HGF ligand dependence, and oncogenic amplification/mutation associations
OpenTargets disease-target associations showing EGFR and KRAS as co-drivers relevant to bypass resistance
CAPTURE trial precedent for cabozantinib post-capmatinib/tepotinib resistance, confirming acquired resistance as active clinical problem
EMBRACE trial (ensartinib) confirming multiple MET exon14 inhibitors in clinical development
concerns: Response durability is limited (median PFS under 9 months) and resistance mechanisms are heterogeneous, complicating single combination strategy prioritization
MET amplification as a biomarker is confounded by copy number thresholds and tumor heterogeneity, risking patient misclassification
D1228 and Y1230 resistance mutations may require type II inhibitors or allosteric agents not yet approved, creating a therapeutic gap
Cross-resistance between capmatinib and tepotinib limits sequential MET inhibitor strategies
requested_followups: Prospective ctDNA profiling at progression to quantify relative frequency of on-target vs bypass resistance in exon14-skipping cohorts
Structural studies comparing type Ib vs type II MET inhibitor binding to D1228N and Y1230C mutant kinase to guide next-generation inhibitor selection
Preclinical co-treatment experiments with MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor in KRAS-bypass resistance models
Biomarker-stratified trial comparing MET inhibitor monotherapy vs MET plus EGFR inhibitor in treatment-naive exon14-skipping NSCLC
scientist_positions.discipline: disease mechanism and causal pathway biology

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated, clinically actionable driver in NSCLC with approved targeted therapies; the scientific frontier lies in resistance biology and combination strategies, not target discovery
key_claims: Capmatinib (GEOMETRY mono-1) and tepotinib (VISION) achieved ~41-68% ORR in MET exon 14-positive NSCLC with median PFS ~5-12 months, establishing regulatory approval

MET exon 14 skipping eliminates the CBL ubiquitin-ligase docking site on exon 14, impairing receptor degradation and causing ligand-independent MET hyperactivation distinct from copy-number-driven amplification

MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKIs is a bypass mechanism not equivalent to exon 14 skipping as a primary driver; predictive value and drug sensitivity profiles differ substantially

Acquired resistance to type Ib MET inhibitors involves on-target kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C (switch-pocket mutations) and off-target bypass via KRAS mutation or EGFR ligand upregulation
Patient stratification must distinguish exon 14 skipping driver tumors (RNA-based or DNA-based NGS) from MET amplification secondary to EGFR resistance to avoid misapplication of MET monotherapy
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records for capmatinib and tepotinib in NSCLC (strong_support score 0.76)
CAPTURE trial (cabozantinib post-capmatinib/tepotinib resistance) cited in hypothesis card
EMBRACE trial (ensartinib in MET exon 14 skipping) cited in hypothesis card
NCBI Gene MET entry confirming RTK biology and oncogenic amplification/mutation associations
concerns: Pivotal trial ORR data cited from hypothesis card without direct PubMed PMIDs; durability figures need citation-level verification
Cross-resistance between type Ib inhibitors and cabozantinib for D1228/Y1230 mutations is incompletely characterized in published literature
MET amplification copy-number thresholds for clinical predictive value remain inconsistently defined across trials
RNA-based exon 14 skipping detection superiority over DNA NGS is asserted in guidelines but sensitivity/specificity data vary by platform
requested_followups: PubMed retrieval of GEOMETRY mono-1 and VISION primary publications for ORR, PFS, and OS with PMIDs
Mechanistic studies comparing degradation kinetics of exon 14-skipped vs wild-type MET receptor in isogenic cell lines
Ranked combination experiment: MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor (RAS bypass) vs MET inhibitor plus EGFR antibody, with resistance mutation-stratified endpoints
Liquid biopsy ctDNA studies quantifying D1228N/Y1230C emergence kinetics on capmatinib/tepotinib to inform early switch strategies

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated, clinically actionable driver in NSCLC with approved selective MET inhibitors, distinct mechanistically and predictively from MET amplification, but limited by acquired resistance and patient-selection complexity
key_claims: Capmatinib and tepotinib are FDA-approved for METex14-skipping NSCLC with ~40-68% ORR in treatment-naive patients and ~40% in pretreated cohorts
METex14 skipping impairs CBL-mediated ubiquitination of Y1003, prolonging receptor half-life and constitutive signaling independent of gene copy number
MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKI is mechanistically distinct and predicts weaker single-agent MET inhibitor response than primary METex14 skipping
Secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C confer resistance to type Ib MET inhibitors; bypass resistance via KRAS mutation or EGFR upregulation activates parallel RAS-MAPK signaling
Patient stratification must distinguish primary METex14 driver tumors from MET amplification arising post-EGFR-TKI using liquid biopsy or tissue NGS with copy number co-assessment
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets: capmatinib and tepotinib both carry Approval status for NSCLC, confirming clinical precedence
OpenTargets: MET association with NSCLC supported by disease-target linkage; EGFR and KRAS top-ranked co-targets relevant to bypass resistance
NCBI Gene MET: RTK with HGF ligand, proto-oncogene with established amplification and mutation roles in cancer
Hypothesis card: CAPTURE trial and EMBRACE trial directly address resistance and alternative inhibitor strategies in METex14 NSCLC
OpenTargets: KRAS association score 0.842 and EGFR 0.888 in NSCLC corroborate bypass resistance pathway relevance
concerns: Response durability is limited; median PFS ~12 months in treatment-naive METex14 cohorts, with no OS benefit demonstrated in randomized trials
D1228 and Y1230 resistance mutations are not addressable by current approved type Ib inhibitors; next-generation options (cabozantinib, type II inhibitors) lack robust phase III data
MET amplification as a co-occurring alteration may confound METex14 predictive biomarker interpretation in real-world NGS panels
Cross-resistance between capmatinib and tepotinib is near-complete, limiting sequential single-agent strategies
requested_followups: Rank combination experiments: MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor (addresses RAS bypass) > MET inhibitor plus EGFR-TKI (addresses EGFR upregulation bypass) > type II MET inhibitor (addresses D1228/Y1230) > MET inhibitor plus MEK inhibitor
Validate D1228N/Y1230C structural models to identify type II inhibitor binding compatibility using cryo-EM or co-crystal data
Prospectively define MET amplification copy number threshold (GCN≥10 or MET/CEP7≥2) that predicts single-agent MET inhibitor response independent of exon 14 status
Establish ctDNA-based METex14 detection sensitivity benchmarks versus tissue NGS to reduce false-negative patient exclusion

scientist_positions.position: As safety_agent I challenge several efficacy and translational overclaims in the hypothesis card while acknowledging the established clinical precedence for MET exon 14 skipping as a therapeutic target.
key_claims: Response rates for capmatinib/tepotinib in MET exon 14-positive NSCLC are meaningful (~40-50% ORR) but durability is limited (median PFS ~5-7 months), which must not be overstated
MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification are mechanistically distinct biomarkers with poor cross-predictive value; conflating them risks patient misselection
Acquired resistance via D1228N/H and Y1230C mutations and bypass via KRAS/EGFR is well-documented and limits single-agent utility
Combination strategies addressing resistance lack prospective safety validation and carry overlapping toxicity risks
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval data confirms capmatinib and tepotinib are approved for NSCLC, supporting clinical precedence
CAPTURE trial reference in hypothesis card confirms resistance to first-line MET inhibitors is a real clinical problem
OpenTargets EGFR and KRAS high association scores (0.888, 0.842) corroborate bypass resistance pathway relevance
NCBI Gene MET entry confirms RTK biology and oncogenic amplification/mutation mechanisms
concerns: ORR and PFS figures cited in literature vary by line of therapy and biomarker selection method (RNA vs DNA); pooling these without stratification inflates apparent efficacy

MET amplification as secondary EGFR resistance mechanism does not predict response to MET inhibitor monotherapy equivalently to exon 14 skipping; this distinction must be explicit in patient stratification
Combination regimens (e.g., MET inhibitor plus EGFR-TKI) have additive hepatotoxicity and QTc risks not adequately addressed in the hypothesis
Preclinical resistance models may not recapitulate clonal heterogeneity seen in clinical specimens; ranked validation experiments should include patient-derived organoids or ctDNA monitoring
requested_followups: Provide line-of-therapy-stratified ORR and PFS data separately for capmatinib and tepotinib pivotal trials
Clarify biomarker detection method (RNA-based vs DNA-based) used in each cited trial and its impact on prevalence estimates
Specify safety monitoring plan for hepatotoxicity in proposed MET plus EGFR-TKI combination experiments
Define criteria distinguishing de novo MET exon 14 skipping driver tumors from MET amplification arising as acquired EGFR resistance before enrolling combination studies

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated oncogenic driver in NSCLC with approved type Ib MET inhibitors capmatinib and tepotinib; the mechanistic and resistance landscape is well-characterized but combination strategies remain unvalidated clinically
key_claims: Capmatinib and tepotinib are FDA/EMA-approved for METex14-skipping NSCLC with ORRs ~40-68% in treatment-naive patients but median PFS ~12 months

METex14 skipping eliminates the CBL-binding juxtamembrane degron encoded by exon 14, impairing receptor ubiquitination and degradation, causing constitutive signaling distinct from copy-number-driven MET amplification
MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKIs is a bypass mechanism not a primary driver; METex14 tumors rarely co-harbor EGFR mutations, making these non-overlapping patient populations

Acquired resistance to type Ib inhibitors is dominated by secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C that sterically clash with type Ib binding mode, plus bypass via KRAS mutation or EGFR ligand upregulation
Patient stratification requires RNA-based or DNA-based assays detecting the splice event; high MET copy number alone is insufficient to predict type Ib inhibitor response
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records confirming capmatinib and tepotinib approvals for NSCLC
NCBI Gene MET entry confirming RTK family membership and oncogenic mutation associations
OpenTargets EGFR and KRAS high association scores for NSCLC confirming bypass pathway relevance
CAPTURE trial precedent for cabozantinib post-type-Ib resistance supporting kinase domain mutation biology
concerns: ORR and PFS data cited are from pivotal trials; real-world durability may be lower and cross-trial comparison is confounded by line of therapy
D1228 and Y1230 resistance mutation frequencies are derived from small post-progression cohorts; relative prevalence vs bypass resistance is uncertain
Combination strategies (e.g., MET inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor or SHP2 inhibitor) lack randomized efficacy data; preclinical synergy has not translated to approved regimens
RNA splicing assay sensitivity varies by platform; liquid biopsy ctDNA detection of splice variants has lower sensitivity than tissue, risking patient misclassification
requested_followups: Provide ranked preclinical evidence for MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor combinations against D1228N/Y1230C resistance models
Clarify whether ensartinib (EMBRACE trial) activity profile differs mechanistically from capmatinib/tepotinib given its multi-kinase profile
Quantify co-mutation frequency of KRAS in METex14-skipping tumors at baseline vs at progression to establish bypass resistance prevalence
Assess whether type II MET inhibitors (e.g., cabozantinib, glesatinib) overcome D1228/Y1230 mutations via distinct binding mode

scientist_positions.position: Pathway and cellular evidence strongly support MET exon 14 skipping as a bona fide oncogenic driver in NSCLC, with mechanistically distinct biology from MET amplification, and the resistance landscape is well-characterized enough to prioritize combination experiments.
key_claims: MET exon 14 skipping eliminates the CBL-binding juxtamembrane degron, prolonging receptor half-life and constitutive signaling independent of gene copy number
Capmatinib and tepotinib show ~40-68% ORR in MET exon14-positive treatment-naive NSCLC with median PFS ~12 months, establishing clinical precedence
MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKI is mechanistically distinct: copy-number driven bypass, not degron loss, predicting weaker single-agent MET inhibitor response
Secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C impair type Ib inhibitor binding; cabozantinib (type II) retains partial activity per CAPTURE trial rationale
Bypass resistance via KRAS mutation or EGFR upregulation reactivates RAS-MAPK downstream of MET, requiring vertical or horizontal combination strategies
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records confirm capmatinib and tepotinib approved for NSCLC, corroborating clinical precedence
NCBI Gene MET entry confirms juxtamembrane regulatory domain and RTK family membership underpinning degron biology
CAPTURE trial (cabozantinib post-capmatinib/tepotinib) provides direct clinical evidence for type II inhibitor utility after resistance
EMBRACE trial (ensartinib in MET exon14) extends inhibitor class evidence beyond type Ib agents
OpenTargets EGFR and KRAS high association scores with NSCLC corroborate bypass resistance pathway relevance
concerns: Durability data remain limited; median PFS ~12 months suggests most patients develop resistance within 1 year, weakening long-term efficacy claims
Cross-response between exon14-skipping and amplification cohorts is inconsistently reported across trials, complicating biomarker boundary claims
Mechanistic evidence for EGFR upregulation as a resistance route is largely preclinical; clinical validation in post-MET-inhibitor biopsies is sparse
Distinguishing true exon14-skipping driver tumors from co-occurring low-level MET amplification requires rigorous ctDNA and FISH co-testing not uniformly applied
requested_followups: Serial ctDNA profiling in capmatinib/tepotinib-treated patients to quantify relative frequency of D1228, Y1230 vs KRAS vs EGFR resistance mechanisms
Isogenic cell line models expressing D1228N or Y1230C to confirm differential sensitivity to type Ib vs type II vs bivalent MET inhibitors
Co-immunoprecipitation and ubiquitination assays in exon14-skipping vs wild-type MET cells to directly quantify CBL-mediated degradation difference
Combination screen of MET inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor or MEK inhibitor in MET exon14-skipping PDX models with acquired EGFR upregulation

scientist_positions.position: The hypothesis card correctly frames MET exon 14 skipping as an established target in NSCLC, but several mechanistic and evidentiary gaps weaken the synthesis as presented.
key_claims: MET exon 14 skipping causes impaired CBL-mediated ubiquitination and receptor degradation, leading to sustained MET signaling independent of amplification
Capmatinib and tepotinib show ~40-68% ORR in treatment-naive MET exon 14-positive NSCLC with median PFS ~5-12 months, establishing clinical precedence
MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification are mechanistically distinct: the former is a primary driver mutation; the latter is frequently a secondary resistance mechanism to EGFR TKIs
Acquired resistance involves on-target secondary kinase domain mutations (D1228N/H, Y1230C) and off-target bypass via KRAS mutation or EGFR upregulation
Patient stratification must distinguish primary MET exon 14 skipping driver tumors from MET amplification arising as EGFR TKI resistance, as these have different therapeutic implications
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets confirms capmatinib and tepotinib approvals specifically for NSCLC, corroborating clinical precedence
CAPTURE trial reference in hypothesis card directly addresses acquired resistance to first-line MET inhibitors
NCBI Gene MET entry confirms RTK biology and role of MET amplification across cancers
OpenTargets association scores confirm EGFR and KRAS as top co-drivers in NSCLC, consistent with bypass resistance mechanisms
concerns: Specific ORR and PFS figures cited (40-68%, 5-12 months) are not directly sourced in the scored evidence digest and require primary trial citation (GEOMETRY mono-1, VISION) for verification
The mechanistic claim that exon 14 skipping specifically impairs CBL-binding domain ubiquitination is biologically sound but not explicitly supported by any evidence source in the digest

Cross-resistance between capmatinib and tepotinib for on-target mutations is asserted but not evidenced; the CAPTURE trial tests cabozantinib post-resistance, implying incomplete cross-resistance but this is not mechanistically resolved
Stratification strategy conflating MET copy number gain with true amplification versus polysomy is a known clinical pitfall not addressed in the hypothesis card
requested_followups: Provide primary trial citations (GEOMETRY mono-1, VISION) with specific ORR and PFS data stratified by treatment line to substantiate response rate claims
Clarify whether MET FISH-based amplification thresholds used in EGFR-resistance settings are validated to distinguish true gene amplification from polysomy in the stratification strategy

Specify ranked combination experiments: e.g., MET inhibitor plus EGFR TKI for EGFR bypass resistance, MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor for on-target resistance, and MET inhibitor plus KRAS G12C inhibitor for KRAS bypass
Address whether liquid biopsy ctDNA detection of exon 14 skipping has equivalent sensitivity to tissue-based NGS for patient selection, as this affects stratification feasibility

agreements: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated, clinically actionable oncogenic driver in NSCLC with FDA-approved targeted therapies (capmatinib, tepotinib); this is not a new target discovery
The mechanistic basis is loss of CBL ubiquitin-ligase docking site encoded by exon 14, impairing receptor degradation and causing sustained MET signaling

MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification are biologically and predictively distinct; amplification secondary to EGFR-TKI resistance is a bypass mechanism with lower single-agent MET inhibitor response rates
Acquired resistance mechanisms include on-target kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C and off-target bypass via KRAS mutation and EGFR upregulation
Patient stratification distinguishing exon 14 skipping driver tumors from secondary MET amplification is essential to avoid misapplication of MET monotherapy
Combination strategies targeting bypass resistance (MET plus SHP2 or MET plus EGFR inhibition) are mechanistically prioritized for validation
disagreements: Median PFS range cited inconsistently across agents: one agent cites 5.4-8.5 months, another 5-12 months; the upper bound of 12 months is not uniformly supported without PMID-level verification
ORR lower bound varies between agents (41% vs 40%) reflecting rounding or cohort differences; treatment-naive vs pretreated subgroup ORR distinctions are not uniformly applied across all positions
Degree of cross-resistance between capmatinib, tepotinib, and cabozantinib for D1228/Y1230 mutations is characterized with differing confidence levels; one agent flags this as incompletely published
overclaims: Stating median PFS up to 12 months without a cited PMID; the upper bound is not consistently supported by the evidence provided and risks overstating durability
Asserting RNA-based NGS superiority over DNA NGS for exon 14 skipping detection as established fact; sensitivity and specificity vary by platform and this remains an active methodological question
Framing SHP2 inhibitor combination as mechanistically prioritized without preclinical or clinical data cited in the evidence base; this is a hypothesis, not a supported claim
required_revisions: Provide PMIDs for GEOMETRY mono-1 and VISION primary publications to anchor ORR and PFS figures; remove or qualify the 12-month PFS upper bound until citation-verified
Explicitly stratify ORR and PFS by treatment-naive vs pretreated cohorts in all quantitative claims to avoid conflation
Qualify cross-resistance characterization between type Ib inhibitors and cabozantinib for D1228/Y1230 as incompletely characterized pending published resistance profiling data
Reframe SHP2 inhibitor combination as a hypothesis requiring ranked preclinical validation rather than a mechanistically established priority

Clarify that MET amplification copy-number thresholds for predictive value are not uniformly defined and that platform-dependent variability in exon 14 skipping detection must be acknowledged in stratification strategies

output.critique: The hypothesis correctly frames MET as clinically precedented in NSCLC, but several evidence links remain unsupported: ClinicalTrials.gov entries for capmatinib and tepotinib in NSCLC and SCLC are cited without claim-specific supporting text. KRAS evidence is included but mechanistically tangential to the MET-NSCLC claim. The NCBI Gene summary for MET does not mention NSCLC specifically, only renal, hepatocellular, and head-and-neck cancers, creating a weak anchor for the primary claim. Safety data from openFDA for tepotinib, capmatinib, and savolitinib are acknowledged but not integrated into the safety liability narrative. Resistance mutation variants (D1228N, D1228H, Y1230C) are referenced but their clinical frequency and actionability are not quantified. The readiness score of 89 may be inflated given 14 of 35 evidence items are labeled irrelevant.
output.recommended_fix: Remove or clearly annotate KRAS evidence as contextual background rather than primary support. Replace unsupported ClinicalTrials.gov links with specific trial identifiers and outcome summaries. Explicitly cite NCBI or peer-reviewed sources confirming MET alteration prevalence in NSCLC. Integrate openFDA safety signals into a structured safety liability section. Quantify resistance mutation frequencies from pivotal trials. Recalculate readiness score excluding irrelevant evidence items.
output.claim_boundary: MET is a clinically validated target in MET exon 14-altered and MET-amplified NSCLC with approved inhibitors; claims about novel mechanisms, combination strategies, or resistance biology require additional prospective clinical evidence before strong conclusions can be drawn.

hypothesis_card.title: MET clinical-precedence and mechanism review for non-small cell lung cancer
hypothesis_card.hypothesis: MET has established or clinically precedented target-disease grounding for non-small cell lung cancer. The output should not present this as a new target discovery. Relevant clinical literature titles include: Vebreltinib plus EGFR-TKI for EGFR-mutated NSCLC with MET-driven resistance: A real-world study of Chinese patients.; A Phase II, Open Label, Single-Arm Study on the Efficacy of Cabozantinib in Patients With Advanced/Metastatic Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer Harboring MET Exon 14 Alterations who Developed Acquired Resistance to Tepotinib or Capmatinib (CAPTURE Trial).; Ensartinib for advanced or metastatic non-small-cell lung cancer with MET exon 14 skipping mutations (EMBRACE): amulti-center, single-arm, phase 2 trial.. The remaining scientific work is to resolve mechanism details, response or resistance biology, safety liabilities, and patient-selection strategy; this is not a claim that a new target has been discovered.

evidence.text: NCBI Gene MET: This gene encodes a member of the receptor tyrosine kinase family of proteins and the product of the proto-oncogene MET. The encoded preproprotein is proteolytically processed to generate alpha and beta subunits that are linked via disulfide bonds to form the mature receptor. Further processing of the beta subunit results in the formation of the M10 peptide, which has been shown to reduce lung fibrosis. Binding of its ligand, hepatocyte growth factor, induces dimerization and activation of the receptor, which plays a role in cellular survival, embryogenesis, and cellular migration and invasion. Mutations in this gene are associated with papillary renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, and various head and neck cancers. Amplification and overexpression of this gene are also associated with multiple human cancers. [provided by RefSeq, May 2016]

evidence.text: NCBI Gene KRAS: This gene, a Kirsten ras oncogene homolog from the mammalian ras gene family, encodes a protein that is a member of the small GTPase superfamily. A single amino acid substitution is responsible for an activating mutation. The transforming protein that results is implicated in various malignancies, including lung adenocarcinoma, mucinous adenoma, ductal carcinoma of the pancreas and colorectal carcinoma. Alternative splicing leads to variants encoding two isoforms that differ in the C-terminal region. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008]

hits.name: non-small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A group of at least three distinct histological types of lung cancer, including non-small cell squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Non-small cell lung carcinomas have a poor response to conventional chemotherapy.

hits.name: non-small cell squamous lung carcinoma
hits.description: A squamous cell carcinoma that arises from the lung. It is characterized by the presence of large malignant cells. It includes the clear cell and papillary variants of squamous cell carcinoma.

hits.name: lung cancer
hits.description: A malignant neoplasm involving the lung.

hits.name: large cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A poorly differentiated non-small cell lung carcinoma composed of large polygonal cells without evidence of glandular or squamous differentiation. There is a male predilection.

hits.name: combined small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A morphologic variant of small cell lung carcinoma in combination with a non-small cell carcinoma.

hits.name: non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: Type of epithelial lung cancer arising from glandular origin.

hits.name: lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: A carcinoma that arises from the lung and is characterized by the presence of malignant glandular epithelial cells. There is a male predilection with a male to female ratio of 2:1. Usually lung adenocarcinoma is asymptomatic and is identified through screening studies or as an incidental radiologic finding. If clinical symptoms are present they include shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, and fever. Tobacco smoke is a known risk factor.

hits.name: Lung Sarcomatoid Carcinoma
hits.description: A rare, aggressive, poorly differentiated, non-small cell lung carcinoma characterized by the presence of a sarcomatoid component often associated with giant cell differentiation. There is a male to female ratio of 4:1. Clinical symptoms include cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, progressive dyspnea and fever secondary to recurrent pneumonia. Cigarette smoking is a major risk factor.

hits.name: Optic disc pallor
hits.description: A pale yellow discoloration of the optic disc (the area of the optic nerve head in the retina). The optic disc normally has a pinkish hue with a central yellowish depression.

hits.name: cone-rod synaptic disorder syndrome, congenital nonprogressive
GCGR-related hyperglucagonemia
hits.description: A rare tumor of pancreas caused by mutations in the GCGR gene characterized by pancreatic alpha cell hyperplasia, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors and markedly increased serum glucagon levels in the absence of a glucagonoma syndrome. Clinical manifestations may include abdominal pain, pancreatitis, fatigue, diarrhea, and diabetes mellitus.

hits.name: night blindness
hits.description: Inability to see clearly in dim light.

hits.name: Hyperhidrosis
hits.description: Abnormal excessive perspiration (sweating) despite the lack of appropriate stimuli like hot and humid weather.

hits.name: disease progression measurement

hits.name: pituitary hormone deficiency, combined, 6
hits.description: Any combined pituitary hormone deficiencies, genetic form in which the cause of the disease is a mutation in the OTX2 gene.

hits.name: adult-onset non-insulinoma persistent hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia
hits.description: Diazoxide-resistant hyperinsulism (DRH) is form of congenital isolated hyperinsulinism caused by an abnormal insulin production by b-cells in the pancreas that can be diffuse or focal and is characterized by an excessive/ uncontrolled insulin secretion (inappropriate for the level of glycemia), recurrent episodes of profound hypoglycemia and resistance to medical management with diazoxide

hits.name: diabetes, deafness, developmental delay, and short stature syndrome
hypochromic anemia
hits.description: Anemia caused by the reduction of hemoglobin in relation to the red cell volume. As a result, the red cells have an area of central pallor which is increased in size. The leading cause is iron deficiency.

hits.name: benign soft tissue neoplasm
hits.description: A non-metastasizing neoplasm that arises from the soft tissue.

hits.name: level of aldehyde dehydrogenase X, mitochondrial in blood serum
hits.description: The amount of a aldehyde dehydrogenase X, mitochondrial when measured in blood serum.

hits.name: leucine-induced hypoglycemia
hits.description: Rare cause of hypoglycemia and is described as a condition in which symptomatic hypoglycemia is provoked by high protein feedings. Hypoglycemia is also elicited by administration of oral or intravenous infusions of a single amino acid, leucine. [http://www.uniprot.org/diseases/DI-01896]

hits.name: isolated growth hormone deficiency type IA
hits.description: An isolated growth hormone deficiency characterized by autosomal recessive inheritance of severe dwarfism with onset by 6 months of age and variable development of antibodies to growth hormone following exogenous supplementation that has material basis in null mutations in the GH1 gene on chromosome 17q23.3.

evidence.text: OpenTargets_get_disease_id_description_by_name returned OpenTargets search hits: small cell lung carcinoma: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is a highly aggressive malignant neoplasm, accounting for 10-15% of lung cancer cases, characterized byrapid growth, and early metastasis. SCLC usually manifests as a large hilar mass with bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy presenting clinically with chest pain, persistent cough, dyspnea, wheezing, hoarseness, hemoptysis, loss of appetite, weight loss, and neurological and endocrine paraneoplastic syndromes. SCLC is primarily reported in elderly people with a history of long-term tobacco exposure.; non-small cell lung carcinoma: A group of at least three distinct histological types of lung cancer, including non-small cell squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Non-small cell lung carcinomas have a poor response to conventional chemotherapy.; combined small cell lung carcinoma: A morphologic variant of small cell lung carcinoma in combination with a non-small cell carcinoma.

hits.name: small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is a highly aggressive malignant neoplasm, accounting for 10-15% of lung cancer cases, characterized byrapid growth, and early metastasis. SCLC usually manifests as a large hilar mass with bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy presenting clinically with chest pain, persistent cough, dyspnea, wheezing, hoarseness, hemoptysis, loss of appetite, weight loss, and neurological and endocrine paraneoplastic syndromes. SCLC is primarily reported in elderly people with a history of long-term tobacco exposure.

hits.name: non-small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A group of at least three distinct histological types of lung cancer, including non-small cell squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Non-small cell lung carcinomas have a poor response to conventional chemotherapy.

hits.name: combined small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A morphologic variant of small cell lung carcinoma in combination with a non-small cell carcinoma.

hits.name: small cell carcinoma
hits.description: A neuroendocrine carcinoma composed of small malignant cells which are often said to resemble "oat cells" under the microscope. Small cell carcinoma most often affects the lungs. Clinically, this is often a rapidly growing cancer that spreads to distant sites early.

hits.name: squamous cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A carcinoma arising from squamous bronchial epithelial cells. It may be keratinizing or non-keratinizing. Keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma is characterized by the presence of keratinization, pearl formation, and/or intercellular bridges. Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma is characterized by the absence of keratinization, pearl formation, and intercellular bridges. Cigarette smoking and arsenic exposure are strongly associated with squamous cell lung carcinoma.

hits.name: Bladder Small Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma
hits.description: A highly aggressive carcinoma, histologically resembling small cell lung carcinoma. In most cases it is associated with carcinoma in situ.

hits.name: non-small cell squamous lung carcinoma
hits.description: A squamous cell carcinoma that arises from the lung. It is characterized by the presence of large malignant cells. It includes the clear cell and papillary variants of squamous cell carcinoma.

hits.name: squamous cell carcinoma
hits.description: A carcinoma arising from squamous epithelial cells. Morphologically, it is characterized by the proliferation of atypical, often pleomorphic squamous cells. Squamous cell carcinomas are graded by the degree of cellular differentiation as well, moderately, or poorly differentiated. Well differentiated carcinomas are usually associated with keratin production and the presence of intercellular bridges between adjacent cells. Representative examples are lung squamous cell carcinoma, skin squamous cell carcinoma, and cervical squamous cell carcinoma.

hits.name: Gastric Small Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma
hits.description: An aggressive, high-grade and poorly differentiated carcinoma with neuroendocrine differentiation that arises from the stomach. It is characterized by the presence of malignant small cells.

hits.name: lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: A carcinoma that arises from the lung and is characterized by the presence of malignant glandular epithelial cells. There is a male predilection with a male to female ratio of 2:1. Usually lung adenocarcinoma is asymptomatic and is identified through screening studies or as an incidental radiologic finding. If clinical symptoms are present they include shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, and fever. Tobacco smoke is a known risk factor.

hits.name: Lung Giant Cell Carcinoma
hits.description: A morphologic variant of lung sarcomatoid carcinoma characterized by the presence of mononuclear and multinucleated pleomorphic neoplastic giant cells that lack cohesion.

hits.name: lung carcinoid tumor
hits.description: A neuroendocrine neoplasm that arises from the lung. It is characterized by the presence of uniform polygonal cells with small or moderate amount of cytoplasm and inconspicuous nucleoli. The cells are usually arranged in organoid and trabecular patterns. It is classified as typical or atypical carcinoid tumor based on the number of mitotic figures and the absence or presence of necrosis. Atypical carcinoid tumors have a worse prognosis.

hits.name: Combined Lung Carcinoma
hits.description: A lung carcinoma characterized by the presence of large or small neuroendocrine carcinoma cells in combination with malignant glandular or squamous epithelial cells.

hits.name: pulmonary large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
hits.description: A large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma that involves the lung(s).

hits.name: non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: Type of epithelial lung cancer arising from glandular origin.

hits.name: interstitial lung disease
hits.description: A diverse group of lung diseases that affect the lung parenchyma. They are characterized by an initial inflammation of pulmonary alveoli that extends to the interstitium and beyond leading to diffuse pulmonary fibrosis. Interstitial lung diseases are classified by their etiology (known or unknown causes), and radiological-pathological features.

hits.name: adenosquamous lung carcinoma
hits.description: An aggressive carcinoma with a poor prognosis characterized by a presence of both malignant squamous cells and glandular cells.

hits.name: lung sarcoma
hits.description: A malignant mesenchymal neoplasm that arises from the lung. Representative examples include Kaposi sarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, and synovial sarcoma.

hits.name: oral squamous cell carcinoma
hits.description: A squamous cell carcinoma arising from the oral cavity. It affects predominantly adults in their fifth and sixth decades of life and is associated with alcohol and tobacco use. Human papillomavirus is present in approximately half of the cases. It is characterized by a tendency to metastasize early to the lymph nodes. When the tumor is small, patients are often asymptomatic. Physical examination may reveal erythematous or white lesions or plaques. The majority of patients present with signs and symptoms of locally advanced disease including mucosal ulceration, pain, difficulty with speaking, chewing, and swallowing, bleeding, weight loss, and neck swelling. Patients may also present with swollen neck lymph nodes without any symptoms from the oropharyngeal tumor. The most significant prognostic factors are the size of the tumor and the lymph nodes status.

hits.name: lung benign neoplasm
hits.description: A non-metastasizing neoplasm that arises from the lung parenchyma or the bronchi. Representative examples include lung adenoma, lung hamartoma, and endobronchial lipoma.

hits.name: squamous cell neoplasm
hits.description: A neoplasm that is composed of squamous epithelial cells. Squamous cell carcinoma is a representative example.

hits.name: lip and oral cavity squamous cell carcinoma
hits.description: A squamous cell carcinoma arising from the lip or the oral cavity. The oral cavity squamous cell carcinoma usually arises from the buccal mucosa, tongue, or gums. It occurs predominantly in adults who use tobacco and alcohol and has a tendency to metastasize early to lymph nodes.

hits.name: lung colloid adenocarcinoma
hits.description: A morphologic variant of lung adenocarcinoma characterized by the presence of mucin pools containing islands of well differentiated adenocarcinoma cells.

hits.name: lung adenoma
hits.description: A benign, well circumscribed epithelial neoplasm that arises from the bronchus or the lung parenchyma. Representative examples include alveolar adenoma, papillary adenoma, and mucus gland adenoma.

hits.name: adenocarcinoma
hits.description: A common cancer characterized by the presence of malignant glandular cells. Morphologically, adenocarcinomas are classified according to the growth pattern (e.g., papillary, alveolar) or according to the secreting product (e.g., mucinous, serous). Representative examples of adenocarcinoma are ductal and lobular breast carcinoma, lung adenocarcinoma, renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma (hepatoma), colon adenocarcinoma, and prostate adenocarcinoma.

case_profile.objective: Generate a mechanistic hypothesis for MET exon 14 skipping mutation as a therapeutic target in NSCLC. Synthesize: (1) the clinical precedence for MET inhibitors capmatinib and tepotinib in MET exon 14-positive NSCLC, including response rates and durability from pivotal trials; (2) the biological distinction between MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification as predictive biomarkers — mechanism, predictive value, and cross-response; (3) acquired resistance mechanisms including secondary MET kinase domain mutations D1228N, D1228H, and Y1230C, and bypass resistance via KRAS mutation and EGFR upregulation; (4) patient stratification strategies differentiating exon 14 skipping driver tumors from MET amplification secondary to EGFR resistance; and (5) ranked validation experiments for combination strategies addressing the most common resistance mechanisms.

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated oncogenic driver in NSCLC with established mechanistic rationale and regulatory-approved targeted therapies; the scientific frontier lies in resistance biology and rational combination strategies
key_claims: MET exon 14 skipping eliminates the juxtamembrane CBL-binding site encoded by exon 14, impairing ubiquitin-mediated receptor degradation and causing sustained MET signaling independent of ligand dose
Capmatinib (GEOMETRY mono-1) and tepotinib (VISION) achieved ~41-68% ORR in MET exon14-positive NSCLC with median PFS ~5.4-8.5 months, establishing clinical precedence

MET exon 14 skipping is a primary driver alteration; MET amplification arising after EGFR-TKI therapy is a bypass resistance mechanism with distinct biology and lower single-agent MET inhibitor response rates

Secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C sterically clash with type Ib inhibitor binding, conferring on-target resistance; KRAS mutation and EGFR upregulation represent off-target bypass resistance
Combination strategies pairing MET inhibitors with EGFR inhibitors or SHP2 inhibitors are mechanistically prioritized to address bypass resistance in exon 14-skipping tumors
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records for capmatinib and tepotinib in NSCLC confirming regulatory validation
NCBI Gene MET entry confirming RTK biology, HGF ligand dependence, and oncogenic amplification/mutation associations
OpenTargets disease-target associations showing EGFR and KRAS as co-drivers relevant to bypass resistance
CAPTURE trial precedent for cabozantinib post-capmatinib/tepotinib resistance, confirming acquired resistance as active clinical problem
EMBRACE trial (ensartinib) confirming multiple MET exon14 inhibitors in clinical development
concerns: Response durability is limited (median PFS under 9 months) and resistance mechanisms are heterogeneous, complicating single combination strategy prioritization
MET amplification as a biomarker is confounded by copy number thresholds and tumor heterogeneity, risking patient misclassification
D1228 and Y1230 resistance mutations may require type II inhibitors or allosteric agents not yet approved, creating a therapeutic gap
Cross-resistance between capmatinib and tepotinib limits sequential MET inhibitor strategies
requested_followups: Prospective ctDNA profiling at progression to quantify relative frequency of on-target vs bypass resistance in exon14-skipping cohorts
Structural studies comparing type Ib vs type II MET inhibitor binding to D1228N and Y1230C mutant kinase to guide next-generation inhibitor selection
Preclinical co-treatment experiments with MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor in KRAS-bypass resistance models
Biomarker-stratified trial comparing MET inhibitor monotherapy vs MET plus EGFR inhibitor in treatment-naive exon14-skipping NSCLC
scientist_positions.discipline: disease mechanism and causal pathway biology

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated, clinically actionable driver in NSCLC with approved targeted therapies; the scientific frontier lies in resistance biology and combination strategies, not target discovery
key_claims: Capmatinib (GEOMETRY mono-1) and tepotinib (VISION) achieved ~41-68% ORR in MET exon 14-positive NSCLC with median PFS ~5-12 months, establishing regulatory approval

MET exon 14 skipping eliminates the CBL ubiquitin-ligase docking site on exon 14, impairing receptor degradation and causing ligand-independent MET hyperactivation distinct from copy-number-driven amplification

MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKIs is a bypass mechanism not equivalent to exon 14 skipping as a primary driver; predictive value and drug sensitivity profiles differ substantially

Acquired resistance to type Ib MET inhibitors involves on-target kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C (switch-pocket mutations) and off-target bypass via KRAS mutation or EGFR ligand upregulation
Patient stratification must distinguish exon 14 skipping driver tumors (RNA-based or DNA-based NGS) from MET amplification secondary to EGFR resistance to avoid misapplication of MET monotherapy
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records for capmatinib and tepotinib in NSCLC (strong_support score 0.76)
CAPTURE trial (cabozantinib post-capmatinib/tepotinib resistance) cited in hypothesis card
EMBRACE trial (ensartinib in MET exon 14 skipping) cited in hypothesis card
NCBI Gene MET entry confirming RTK biology and oncogenic amplification/mutation associations
concerns: Pivotal trial ORR data cited from hypothesis card without direct PubMed PMIDs; durability figures need citation-level verification
Cross-resistance between type Ib inhibitors and cabozantinib for D1228/Y1230 mutations is incompletely characterized in published literature
MET amplification copy-number thresholds for clinical predictive value remain inconsistently defined across trials
RNA-based exon 14 skipping detection superiority over DNA NGS is asserted in guidelines but sensitivity/specificity data vary by platform
requested_followups: PubMed retrieval of GEOMETRY mono-1 and VISION primary publications for ORR, PFS, and OS with PMIDs
Mechanistic studies comparing degradation kinetics of exon 14-skipped vs wild-type MET receptor in isogenic cell lines
Ranked combination experiment: MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor (RAS bypass) vs MET inhibitor plus EGFR antibody, with resistance mutation-stratified endpoints
Liquid biopsy ctDNA studies quantifying D1228N/Y1230C emergence kinetics on capmatinib/tepotinib to inform early switch strategies

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated, clinically actionable driver in NSCLC with approved selective MET inhibitors, distinct mechanistically and predictively from MET amplification, but limited by acquired resistance and patient-selection complexity
key_claims: Capmatinib and tepotinib are FDA-approved for METex14-skipping NSCLC with ~40-68% ORR in treatment-naive patients and ~40% in pretreated cohorts
METex14 skipping impairs CBL-mediated ubiquitination of Y1003, prolonging receptor half-life and constitutive signaling independent of gene copy number
MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKI is mechanistically distinct and predicts weaker single-agent MET inhibitor response than primary METex14 skipping
Secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C confer resistance to type Ib MET inhibitors; bypass resistance via KRAS mutation or EGFR upregulation activates parallel RAS-MAPK signaling
Patient stratification must distinguish primary METex14 driver tumors from MET amplification arising post-EGFR-TKI using liquid biopsy or tissue NGS with copy number co-assessment
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets: capmatinib and tepotinib both carry Approval status for NSCLC, confirming clinical precedence
OpenTargets: MET association with NSCLC supported by disease-target linkage; EGFR and KRAS top-ranked co-targets relevant to bypass resistance
NCBI Gene MET: RTK with HGF ligand, proto-oncogene with established amplification and mutation roles in cancer
Hypothesis card: CAPTURE trial and EMBRACE trial directly address resistance and alternative inhibitor strategies in METex14 NSCLC
OpenTargets: KRAS association score 0.842 and EGFR 0.888 in NSCLC corroborate bypass resistance pathway relevance
concerns: Response durability is limited; median PFS ~12 months in treatment-naive METex14 cohorts, with no OS benefit demonstrated in randomized trials
D1228 and Y1230 resistance mutations are not addressable by current approved type Ib inhibitors; next-generation options (cabozantinib, type II inhibitors) lack robust phase III data
MET amplification as a co-occurring alteration may confound METex14 predictive biomarker interpretation in real-world NGS panels
Cross-resistance between capmatinib and tepotinib is near-complete, limiting sequential single-agent strategies
requested_followups: Rank combination experiments: MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor (addresses RAS bypass) > MET inhibitor plus EGFR-TKI (addresses EGFR upregulation bypass) > type II MET inhibitor (addresses D1228/Y1230) > MET inhibitor plus MEK inhibitor
Validate D1228N/Y1230C structural models to identify type II inhibitor binding compatibility using cryo-EM or co-crystal data
Prospectively define MET amplification copy number threshold (GCN≥10 or MET/CEP7≥2) that predicts single-agent MET inhibitor response independent of exon 14 status
Establish ctDNA-based METex14 detection sensitivity benchmarks versus tissue NGS to reduce false-negative patient exclusion

scientist_positions.position: As safety_agent I challenge several efficacy and translational overclaims in the hypothesis card while acknowledging the established clinical precedence for MET exon 14 skipping as a therapeutic target.
key_claims: Response rates for capmatinib/tepotinib in MET exon 14-positive NSCLC are meaningful (~40-50% ORR) but durability is limited (median PFS ~5-7 months), which must not be overstated
MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification are mechanistically distinct biomarkers with poor cross-predictive value; conflating them risks patient misselection
Acquired resistance via D1228N/H and Y1230C mutations and bypass via KRAS/EGFR is well-documented and limits single-agent utility
Combination strategies addressing resistance lack prospective safety validation and carry overlapping toxicity risks
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval data confirms capmatinib and tepotinib are approved for NSCLC, supporting clinical precedence
CAPTURE trial reference in hypothesis card confirms resistance to first-line MET inhibitors is a real clinical problem
OpenTargets EGFR and KRAS high association scores (0.888, 0.842) corroborate bypass resistance pathway relevance
NCBI Gene MET entry confirms RTK biology and oncogenic amplification/mutation mechanisms
concerns: ORR and PFS figures cited in literature vary by line of therapy and biomarker selection method (RNA vs DNA); pooling these without stratification inflates apparent efficacy

MET amplification as secondary EGFR resistance mechanism does not predict response to MET inhibitor monotherapy equivalently to exon 14 skipping; this distinction must be explicit in patient stratification
Combination regimens (e.g., MET inhibitor plus EGFR-TKI) have additive hepatotoxicity and QTc risks not adequately addressed in the hypothesis
Preclinical resistance models may not recapitulate clonal heterogeneity seen in clinical specimens; ranked validation experiments should include patient-derived organoids or ctDNA monitoring
requested_followups: Provide line-of-therapy-stratified ORR and PFS data separately for capmatinib and tepotinib pivotal trials
Clarify biomarker detection method (RNA-based vs DNA-based) used in each cited trial and its impact on prevalence estimates
Specify safety monitoring plan for hepatotoxicity in proposed MET plus EGFR-TKI combination experiments
Define criteria distinguishing de novo MET exon 14 skipping driver tumors from MET amplification arising as acquired EGFR resistance before enrolling combination studies

scientist_positions.position: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated oncogenic driver in NSCLC with approved type Ib MET inhibitors capmatinib and tepotinib; the mechanistic and resistance landscape is well-characterized but combination strategies remain unvalidated clinically
key_claims: Capmatinib and tepotinib are FDA/EMA-approved for METex14-skipping NSCLC with ORRs ~40-68% in treatment-naive patients but median PFS ~12 months

METex14 skipping eliminates the CBL-binding juxtamembrane degron encoded by exon 14, impairing receptor ubiquitination and degradation, causing constitutive signaling distinct from copy-number-driven MET amplification
MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKIs is a bypass mechanism not a primary driver; METex14 tumors rarely co-harbor EGFR mutations, making these non-overlapping patient populations

Acquired resistance to type Ib inhibitors is dominated by secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C that sterically clash with type Ib binding mode, plus bypass via KRAS mutation or EGFR ligand upregulation
Patient stratification requires RNA-based or DNA-based assays detecting the splice event; high MET copy number alone is insufficient to predict type Ib inhibitor response
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records confirming capmatinib and tepotinib approvals for NSCLC
NCBI Gene MET entry confirming RTK family membership and oncogenic mutation associations
OpenTargets EGFR and KRAS high association scores for NSCLC confirming bypass pathway relevance
CAPTURE trial precedent for cabozantinib post-type-Ib resistance supporting kinase domain mutation biology
concerns: ORR and PFS data cited are from pivotal trials; real-world durability may be lower and cross-trial comparison is confounded by line of therapy
D1228 and Y1230 resistance mutation frequencies are derived from small post-progression cohorts; relative prevalence vs bypass resistance is uncertain
Combination strategies (e.g., MET inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor or SHP2 inhibitor) lack randomized efficacy data; preclinical synergy has not translated to approved regimens
RNA splicing assay sensitivity varies by platform; liquid biopsy ctDNA detection of splice variants has lower sensitivity than tissue, risking patient misclassification
requested_followups: Provide ranked preclinical evidence for MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor combinations against D1228N/Y1230C resistance models
Clarify whether ensartinib (EMBRACE trial) activity profile differs mechanistically from capmatinib/tepotinib given its multi-kinase profile
Quantify co-mutation frequency of KRAS in METex14-skipping tumors at baseline vs at progression to establish bypass resistance prevalence
Assess whether type II MET inhibitors (e.g., cabozantinib, glesatinib) overcome D1228/Y1230 mutations via distinct binding mode

scientist_positions.position: Pathway and cellular evidence strongly support MET exon 14 skipping as a bona fide oncogenic driver in NSCLC, with mechanistically distinct biology from MET amplification, and the resistance landscape is well-characterized enough to prioritize combination experiments.
key_claims: MET exon 14 skipping eliminates the CBL-binding juxtamembrane degron, prolonging receptor half-life and constitutive signaling independent of gene copy number
Capmatinib and tepotinib show ~40-68% ORR in MET exon14-positive treatment-naive NSCLC with median PFS ~12 months, establishing clinical precedence
MET amplification as secondary resistance to EGFR-TKI is mechanistically distinct: copy-number driven bypass, not degron loss, predicting weaker single-agent MET inhibitor response
Secondary kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C impair type Ib inhibitor binding; cabozantinib (type II) retains partial activity per CAPTURE trial rationale
Bypass resistance via KRAS mutation or EGFR upregulation reactivates RAS-MAPK downstream of MET, requiring vertical or horizontal combination strategies
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets approval records confirm capmatinib and tepotinib approved for NSCLC, corroborating clinical precedence
NCBI Gene MET entry confirms juxtamembrane regulatory domain and RTK family membership underpinning degron biology
CAPTURE trial (cabozantinib post-capmatinib/tepotinib) provides direct clinical evidence for type II inhibitor utility after resistance
EMBRACE trial (ensartinib in MET exon14) extends inhibitor class evidence beyond type Ib agents
OpenTargets EGFR and KRAS high association scores with NSCLC corroborate bypass resistance pathway relevance
concerns: Durability data remain limited; median PFS ~12 months suggests most patients develop resistance within 1 year, weakening long-term efficacy claims
Cross-response between exon14-skipping and amplification cohorts is inconsistently reported across trials, complicating biomarker boundary claims
Mechanistic evidence for EGFR upregulation as a resistance route is largely preclinical; clinical validation in post-MET-inhibitor biopsies is sparse
Distinguishing true exon14-skipping driver tumors from co-occurring low-level MET amplification requires rigorous ctDNA and FISH co-testing not uniformly applied
requested_followups: Serial ctDNA profiling in capmatinib/tepotinib-treated patients to quantify relative frequency of D1228, Y1230 vs KRAS vs EGFR resistance mechanisms
Isogenic cell line models expressing D1228N or Y1230C to confirm differential sensitivity to type Ib vs type II vs bivalent MET inhibitors
Co-immunoprecipitation and ubiquitination assays in exon14-skipping vs wild-type MET cells to directly quantify CBL-mediated degradation difference
Combination screen of MET inhibitor plus EGFR inhibitor or MEK inhibitor in MET exon14-skipping PDX models with acquired EGFR upregulation

scientist_positions.position: The hypothesis card correctly frames MET exon 14 skipping as an established target in NSCLC, but several mechanistic and evidentiary gaps weaken the synthesis as presented.
key_claims: MET exon 14 skipping causes impaired CBL-mediated ubiquitination and receptor degradation, leading to sustained MET signaling independent of amplification
Capmatinib and tepotinib show ~40-68% ORR in treatment-naive MET exon 14-positive NSCLC with median PFS ~5-12 months, establishing clinical precedence
MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification are mechanistically distinct: the former is a primary driver mutation; the latter is frequently a secondary resistance mechanism to EGFR TKIs
Acquired resistance involves on-target secondary kinase domain mutations (D1228N/H, Y1230C) and off-target bypass via KRAS mutation or EGFR upregulation
Patient stratification must distinguish primary MET exon 14 skipping driver tumors from MET amplification arising as EGFR TKI resistance, as these have different therapeutic implications
supporting_evidence_sources: OpenTargets confirms capmatinib and tepotinib approvals specifically for NSCLC, corroborating clinical precedence
CAPTURE trial reference in hypothesis card directly addresses acquired resistance to first-line MET inhibitors
NCBI Gene MET entry confirms RTK biology and role of MET amplification across cancers
OpenTargets association scores confirm EGFR and KRAS as top co-drivers in NSCLC, consistent with bypass resistance mechanisms
concerns: Specific ORR and PFS figures cited (40-68%, 5-12 months) are not directly sourced in the scored evidence digest and require primary trial citation (GEOMETRY mono-1, VISION) for verification
The mechanistic claim that exon 14 skipping specifically impairs CBL-binding domain ubiquitination is biologically sound but not explicitly supported by any evidence source in the digest

Cross-resistance between capmatinib and tepotinib for on-target mutations is asserted but not evidenced; the CAPTURE trial tests cabozantinib post-resistance, implying incomplete cross-resistance but this is not mechanistically resolved
Stratification strategy conflating MET copy number gain with true amplification versus polysomy is a known clinical pitfall not addressed in the hypothesis card
requested_followups: Provide primary trial citations (GEOMETRY mono-1, VISION) with specific ORR and PFS data stratified by treatment line to substantiate response rate claims
Clarify whether MET FISH-based amplification thresholds used in EGFR-resistance settings are validated to distinguish true gene amplification from polysomy in the stratification strategy

Specify ranked combination experiments: e.g., MET inhibitor plus EGFR TKI for EGFR bypass resistance, MET inhibitor plus SHP2 inhibitor for on-target resistance, and MET inhibitor plus KRAS G12C inhibitor for KRAS bypass
Address whether liquid biopsy ctDNA detection of exon 14 skipping has equivalent sensitivity to tissue-based NGS for patient selection, as this affects stratification feasibility

agreements: MET exon 14 skipping is a validated, clinically actionable oncogenic driver in NSCLC with FDA-approved targeted therapies (capmatinib, tepotinib); this is not a new target discovery
The mechanistic basis is loss of CBL ubiquitin-ligase docking site encoded by exon 14, impairing receptor degradation and causing sustained MET signaling

MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification are biologically and predictively distinct; amplification secondary to EGFR-TKI resistance is a bypass mechanism with lower single-agent MET inhibitor response rates
Acquired resistance mechanisms include on-target kinase domain mutations D1228N/H and Y1230C and off-target bypass via KRAS mutation and EGFR upregulation
Patient stratification distinguishing exon 14 skipping driver tumors from secondary MET amplification is essential to avoid misapplication of MET monotherapy
Combination strategies targeting bypass resistance (MET plus SHP2 or MET plus EGFR inhibition) are mechanistically prioritized for validation
disagreements: Median PFS range cited inconsistently across agents: one agent cites 5.4-8.5 months, another 5-12 months; the upper bound of 12 months is not uniformly supported without PMID-level verification
ORR lower bound varies between agents (41% vs 40%) reflecting rounding or cohort differences; treatment-naive vs pretreated subgroup ORR distinctions are not uniformly applied across all positions
Degree of cross-resistance between capmatinib, tepotinib, and cabozantinib for D1228/Y1230 mutations is characterized with differing confidence levels; one agent flags this as incompletely published
overclaims: Stating median PFS up to 12 months without a cited PMID; the upper bound is not consistently supported by the evidence provided and risks overstating durability
Asserting RNA-based NGS superiority over DNA NGS for exon 14 skipping detection as established fact; sensitivity and specificity vary by platform and this remains an active methodological question
Framing SHP2 inhibitor combination as mechanistically prioritized without preclinical or clinical data cited in the evidence base; this is a hypothesis, not a supported claim
required_revisions: Provide PMIDs for GEOMETRY mono-1 and VISION primary publications to anchor ORR and PFS figures; remove or qualify the 12-month PFS upper bound until citation-verified
Explicitly stratify ORR and PFS by treatment-naive vs pretreated cohorts in all quantitative claims to avoid conflation
Qualify cross-resistance characterization between type Ib inhibitors and cabozantinib for D1228/Y1230 as incompletely characterized pending published resistance profiling data
Reframe SHP2 inhibitor combination as a hypothesis requiring ranked preclinical validation rather than a mechanistically established priority

Clarify that MET amplification copy-number thresholds for predictive value are not uniformly defined and that platform-dependent variability in exon 14 skipping detection must be acknowledged in stratification strategies

critique.critique: The hypothesis correctly frames MET as clinically precedented in NSCLC, but several evidence links remain unsupported: ClinicalTrials.gov entries for capmatinib and tepotinib in NSCLC and SCLC are cited without claim-specific supporting text. KRAS evidence is included but mechanistically tangential to the MET-NSCLC claim. The NCBI Gene summary for MET does not mention NSCLC specifically, only renal, hepatocellular, and head-and-neck cancers, creating a weak anchor for the primary claim. Safety data from openFDA for tepotinib, capmatinib, and savolitinib are acknowledged but not integrated into the safety liability narrative. Resistance mutation variants (D1228N, D1228H, Y1230C) are referenced but their clinical frequency and actionability are not quantified. The readiness score of 89 may be inflated given 14 of 35 evidence items are labeled irrelevant.
critique.recommended_fix: Remove or clearly annotate KRAS evidence as contextual background rather than primary support. Replace unsupported ClinicalTrials.gov links with specific trial identifiers and outcome summaries. Explicitly cite NCBI or peer-reviewed sources confirming MET alteration prevalence in NSCLC. Integrate openFDA safety signals into a structured safety liability section. Quantify resistance mutation frequencies from pivotal trials. Recalculate readiness score excluding irrelevant evidence items.
critique.claim_boundary: MET is a clinically validated target in MET exon 14-altered and MET-amplified NSCLC with approved inhibitors; claims about novel mechanisms, combination strategies, or resistance biology require additional prospective clinical evidence before strong conclusions can be drawn.

{
  "title": "MET Inhibition in MET-Altered Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Precedence, Resistance, and Patient Selection",
  "summary": "MET is a clinically validated oncology target in non-small cell lung cancer (N

hypothesis_card.title: MET clinical-precedence and mechanism review for non-small cell lung cancer
hypothesis_card.hypothesis: MET has established or clinically precedented target-disease grounding for non-small cell lung cancer. The output should not present this as a new target discovery. Relevant clinical literature titles include: Vebreltinib plus EGFR-TKI for EGFR-mutated NSCLC with MET-driven resistance: A real-world study of Chinese patients.; A Phase II, Open Label, Single-Arm Study on the Efficacy of Cabozantinib in Patients With Advanced/Metastatic Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer Harboring MET Exon 14 Alterations who Developed Acquired Resistance to Tepotinib or Capmatinib (CAPTURE Trial).; Ensartinib for advanced or metastatic non-small-cell lung cancer with MET exon 14 skipping mutations (EMBRACE): amulti-center, single-arm, phase 2 trial.. The remaining scientific work is to resolve mechanism details, response or resistance biology, safety liabilities, and patient-selection strategy; this is not a claim that a new target has been discovered.

evidence.text: NCBI Gene MET: This gene encodes a member of the receptor tyrosine kinase family of proteins and the product of the proto-oncogene MET. The encoded preproprotein is proteolytically processed to generate alpha and beta subunits that are linked via disulfide bonds to form the mature receptor. Further processing of the beta subunit results in the formation of the M10 peptide, which has been shown to reduce lung fibrosis. Binding of its ligand, hepatocyte growth factor, induces dimerization and activation of the receptor, which plays a role in cellular survival, embryogenesis, and cellular migration and invasion. Mutations in this gene are associated with papillary renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, and various head and neck cancers. Amplification and overexpression of this gene are also associated with multiple human cancers. [provided by RefSeq, May 2016]

hits.name: non-small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A group of at least three distinct histological types of lung cancer, including non-small cell squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Non-small cell lung carcinomas have a poor response to conventional chemotherapy.

hits.name: non-small cell squamous lung carcinoma
hits.description: A squamous cell carcinoma that arises from the lung. It is characterized by the presence of large malignant cells. It includes the clear cell and papillary variants of squamous cell carcinoma.

hits.name: lung cancer
hits.description: A malignant neoplasm involving the lung.

hits.name: large cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A poorly differentiated non-small cell lung carcinoma composed of large polygonal cells without evidence of glandular or squamous differentiation. There is a male predilection.

hits.name: combined small cell lung carcinoma
hits.description: A morphologic variant of small cell lung carcinoma in combination with a non-small cell carcinoma.

hits.name: non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: Type of epithelial lung cancer arising from glandular origin.

hits.name: lung adenocarcinoma
hits.description: A carcinoma that arises from the lung and is characterized by the presence of malignant glandular epithelial cells. There is a male predilection with a male to female ratio of 2:1. Usually lung adenocarcinoma is asymptomatic and is identified through screening studies or as an incidental radiologic finding. If clinical symptoms are present they include shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, and fever. Tobacco smoke is a known risk factor.

hits.name: Lung Sarcomatoid Carcinoma
hits.description: A rare, aggressive, poorly differentiated, non-small cell lung carcinoma characterized by the presence of a sarcomatoid component often associated with giant cell differentiation. There is a male to female ratio of 4:1. Clinical symptoms include cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, progressive dyspnea and fever secondary to recurrent pneumonia. Cigarette smoking is a major risk factor.

hits.name: Optic disc pallor
hits.description: A pale yellow discoloration of the optic disc (the area of the optic nerve head in the retina). The optic disc normally has a pinkish hue with a central yellowish depression.

hits.name: cone-rod synaptic disorder syndrome, congenital nonprogressive
GCGR-related hyperglucagonemia
hits.description: A rare tumor of pancreas caused by mutations in the GCGR gene characterized by pancreatic alpha cell hyperplasia, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors and markedly increased serum glucagon levels in the absence of a glucagonoma syndrome. Clinical manifestations may include abdominal pain, pancreatitis, fatigue, diarrhea, and diabetes mellitus.

hits.name: night blindness
hits.description: Inability to see clearly in dim light.

hits.name: Hyperhidrosis
hits.description: Abnormal excessive perspiration (sweating) despite the lack of appropriate stimuli like hot and humid weather.

hits.name: pituitary hormone deficiency, combined, 6
hits.description: Any combined pituitary hormone deficiencies, genetic form in which the cause of the disease is a mutation in the OTX2 gene.

hits.name: adult-onset non-insulinoma persistent hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia
hits.description: Diazoxide-resistant hyperinsulism (DRH) is form of congenital isolated hyperinsulinism caused by an abnormal insulin production by b-cells in the pancreas that can be diffuse or focal and is characterized by an excessive/ uncontrolled insulin secretion (inappropriate for the level of glycemia), recurrent episodes of profound hypoglycemia and resistance to medical management with diazoxide

hits.name: diabetes, deafness, developmental delay, and short stature syndrome
hypochromic anemia
hits.description: Anemia caused by the reduction of hemoglobin in relation to the red cell volume. As a result, the red cells have an area of central pallor which is increased in size. The leading cause is iron deficiency.

hits.name: benign soft tissue neoplasm
hits.description: A non-metastasizing neoplasm that arises from the soft tissue.

hits.name: level of aldehyde dehydrogenase X, mitochondrial in blood serum
hits.description: The amount of a aldehyde dehydrogenase X, mitochondrial when measured in blood serum.

hits.name: leucine-induced hypoglycemia
hits.description: Rare cause of hypoglycemia and is described as a condition in which symptomatic hypoglycemia is provoked by high protein feedings. Hypoglycemia is also elicited by administration of oral or intravenous infusions of a single amino acid, leucine. [http://www.uniprot.org/diseases/DI-01896]

hits.name: isolated growth hormone deficiency type IA
hits.description: An isolated growth hormone deficiency characterized by autosomal recessive inheritance of severe dwarfism with onset by 6 months of age and variable development of antibodies to growth hormone following exogenous supplementation that has material basis in null mutations in the GH1 gene on chromosome 17q23.3.
